title: Recursion
summary: Solve problems by having functions call themselves on smaller subproblems, naturally expressing hierarchical and self-similar structures.
overview: Recursion reduces a problem to a base case plus recursive calls on smaller inputs. It's the natural fit for trees, nested data, and divide-and-conquer algorithms. In React, components are recursive by nature — a component can render instances of itself for nested data.
coreConcept: Every recursive function needs: (1) a base case that stops the recursion, and (2) a recursive case that makes progress toward the base case. The call stack implicitly stores state at each level. Tail recursion is a special form where the recursive call is the last operation, enabling potential optimization.
problemPatterns: Divide and conquer (merge sort, quick sort)
signals: Problem can be broken into identical subproblems
Problem has 'all possibilities' or 'all paths' flavor

frontendScenarios.title: Recursive React Components
frontendScenarios.description: Nested menus, comment threads, and folder trees render naturally with recursive components. The component renders itself for each child, with depth tracking for indentation.

frontendScenarios.title: Deep Object Transform
frontendScenarios.description: API responses often have camelCase keys needing conversion to snake_case (or vice versa) throughout deeply nested structures. Recursion handles arbitrary depth naturally.

dataThinking: Think of recursion as delegating subproblems to a future version of yourself that has already solved them. Your job is: handle the base case, assume the recursive call works, and combine results. This inductive thinking is the core skill. In React, useEffect cleanup, context propagation, and error boundaries all exhibit recursive or tree-shaped behavior.

naiveApproach.description: Iterative approach with explicit data structures (stack for DFS, queue for BFS). More verbose but avoids stack overflow.

betterApproach.description: Recursive solution is often cleaner and more readable. Use tail recursion or memoization to optimize. For very deep inputs, convert to iterative with explicit stack.
betterApproach.complexity: O(n) time, O(h) space on call stack

visualizationSteps.label: factorial(4) call
visualizationSteps.explanation: factorial(4) = 4 * factorial(3). Pushes factorial(3) onto call stack.

visualizationSteps.label: Unwinding to base case
visualizationSteps.explanation: Stack builds up until base case factorial(1)=1. Now we start returning.

visualizationSteps.label: Returns propagate up
visualizationSteps.explanation: Each frame multiplies: 1→2→6→24. The call stack unwinds, combining results bottom-up.

practiceItems.title: Deep Key Transform
practiceItems.question: Write a function deepTransformKeys(obj, transform) that applies a transform function to every key in a deeply nested object/array structure. Useful for normalizing API responses.
practiceItems.hint1: Handle four cases: null/primitive (return as-is), array (map recursively), object (transform each key and recurse on value).
practiceItems.hint2: Use typeof checks and Array.isArray() to distinguish cases. Object.entries + Object.fromEntries for transformation.

practiceItems.title: Generate All Permutations
practiceItems.question: Generate all permutations of an array. This models generating all possible orderings — useful in testing, configuration generators, or UI 'all combinations' previews.
practiceItems.hint1: For each element, fix it as the first, then recursively permute the rest. Concatenate.
practiceItems.hint2: Base case: array of length 0 or 1 has exactly one permutation (itself).

reflectionPrompts: What is the maximum safe recursion depth in JavaScript, and how would you handle deeply nested data that exceeds it?
How does React's own reconciliation algorithm use recursive-like traversal? What optimizations does it make?
Tail call optimization: what is it, does JavaScript support it, and when does it matter?
When is an iterative solution strictly better than a recursive one, even if recursion is cleaner?
